title: Conditional Input Demand for a Two-Output Technology
learning_objective: Derive conditional input demand from a binding production requirement.
preliminaries: Input prices are \(w_1,w_2>0\). Outputs are \(q=(q_1,q_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Producing \(q\) requires inputs \(z=(z_1,z_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\) satisfying \(z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge (q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\), where \(\sigma>0\).
question: Derive the conditional input demand \(z(w,q)\).
solution: Let \(A(q)=(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\). The cost-minimization problem is \[\min_{z_1,z_2\ge 0} w_1z_1+w_2z_2 \quad \text{s.t.}\quad z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge A(q).\] For \(q\ne 0\), the constraint binds and the optimum is interior. Using logs, the constraint is \((1/3)\log z_1+(1/3)\log z_2\ge \log A(q)\). The FOCs are \(w_1=\lambda/(3z_1)\) and \(w_2=\lambda/(3z_2)\), so \(w_1z_1=w_2z_2\) and \(z_1=(w_2/w_1)z_2\). The binding constraint implies \(z_1z_2=A(q)^3=(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/\sigma}\). Therefore \[z_1(w,q)=\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)},\qquad z_2(w,q)=\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] If \(q=(0,0)\), the same formula gives \(z=(0,0)\).

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion from the production theory question.

validation.notes: FOCs and binding constraint independently checked.

title: Cost Function for a Multi-Output Requirement
learning_objective: Compute the cost function by substituting conditional input demands.
preliminaries: Use the technology \(z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge (q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\) and input prices \(w_1,w_2>0\). The conditional input demands are \(z_1=\sqrt{w_2/w_1}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\) and \(z_2=\sqrt{w_1/w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\).
question: Derive the cost function \(C(w,q)\).
solution: The cost function is the minimized value \(C(w,q)=w_1z_1(w,q)+w_2z_2(w,q)\). Substitution gives \[C(w,q)=w_1\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}+w_2\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] Both terms equal \(\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\), so \[C(w,q)=2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] This also gives zero cost at \(q=(0,0)\), as expected.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion using the validated conditional demand from part A.

validation.notes: Cost substitution and homogeneity in input prices verified.

title: Output Supply with \(\sigma=3/2\)
learning_objective: Use a cost function to derive profit-maximizing outputs.
preliminaries: Let output prices be \(p_1,p_2>0\), input prices be \(w_1,w_2>0\), and set \(\sigma=3/2\). The cost function is \(C(w,q)=2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\).
question: Derive the profit-maximizing output vector \(q^*(p,w)\).
solution: The firm solves \[\max_{q_1,q_2\ge 0} p_1q_1+p_2q_2-2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2}).\] The objective is strictly concave in \((q_1,q_2)\) because \(q_i^{3/2}\) is strictly convex and enters with a negative coefficient. Since \(p_i>0\), the optimum is interior. The FOCs are \[p_i-3\sqrt{w_1w_2}q_i^{1/2}=0\quad i=1,2.\] Hence \[q_i^*(p,w)=\frac{p_i^2}{9w_1w_2}\quad i=1,2.\]

source.notes: Subpart split from the unconditional input demand calculation.

validation.notes: Strict concavity and FOCs independently checked.

title: Unconditional Input Demand with \(\sigma=3/2\)
learning_objective: Obtain unconditional input demand by composing conditional demand with output supply.
preliminaries: For \(\sigma=3/2\), conditional input demands are \(z_1(w,q)=\sqrt{w_2/w_1}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\) and \(z_2(w,q)=\sqrt{w_1/w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\). Profit-maximizing outputs are \(q_i^*(p,w)=p_i^2/(9w_1w_2)\).
question: Derive the unconditional input demands \(x_1^*(p,w)\) and \(x_2^*(p,w)\).
solution: Unconditional input demand is conditional demand evaluated at the profit-maximizing output: \(x^*(p,w)=z(w,q^*(p,w))\). Since \((q_i^*)^{3/2}=p_i^3/(27(w_1w_2)^{3/2})\), \[q_1^{*3/2}+q_2^{*3/2}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}.\] Therefore \[x_1^*(p,w)=\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1^2w_2},\] and \[x_2^*(p,w)=\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1w_2^2}.\]

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion focused only on unconditional input demand.

validation.notes: Composition and exponent arithmetic independently verified.

title: Input Price Derivative of Profit
learning_objective: Apply Hotelling's lemma to an input price derivative.
preliminaries: For a differentiable profit function \(\pi(p,w)=\max_q\{p\cdot q-C(w,q)\}\), Hotelling's lemma gives \(\partial \pi/\partial w_i=-x_i^*(p,w)\), where \(x_i^*\) is unconditional demand for input \(i\). In this problem \(x_1^*(p,w)=(p_1^3+p_2^3)/(27w_1^2w_2)\).
question: Give \(\partial \pi(p,w)/\partial w_1\) without first deriving and differentiating a closed-form profit function.
solution: By Hotelling's lemma, the derivative of maximum profit with respect to an input price equals minus the profit-maximizing use of that input. Therefore \[\frac{\partial \pi(p,w)}{\partial w_1}=-x_1^*(p,w)=-\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1^2w_2}.\] The sign is negative because increasing an input price lowers profit at the rate at which the firm uses that input, holding the optimized choice fixed by the envelope theorem.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion emphasizing the envelope argument requested by the exam.

validation.notes: Hotelling sign and input demand substitution checked.

title: The Work-Minimization Problem
learning_objective: Formulate a dual work-minimization problem with labor entering preferences.
preliminaries: A consumer chooses \(x\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\) and work hours \(h\ge 0\). Utility \(u(x,h)\) is continuous, strictly increasing in each component of \(x\), and weakly decreasing in \(h\). The wage is normalized to one, so the budget constraint is \(p\cdot x\le h\).
question: For target utility \(\bar u\), write the work-minimization problem and define the minimal-work function.
solution: The work-minimization problem is \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0,h\ge 0}\{h:u(x,h)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h\}.\] The value \(m(p,\bar u)\) is the minimal number of work hours needed to afford a bundle that attains utility at least \(\bar u\). Since utility is weakly decreasing in \(h\), any feasible pair with \(h>p\cdot x\) can reduce \(h\) down to \(p\cdot x\) without hurting feasibility or lowering utility. Thus an equivalent reduced problem is \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0}\{p\cdot x:u(x,p\cdot x)\ge \bar u\}.\]

source.notes: Converted as a standalone formulation subpart.

validation.notes: Problem formulation and binding-work reduction checked.

title: Minimal Work Is Nondecreasing in Prices
learning_objective: Prove price monotonicity of the minimal-work function by set inclusion.
preliminaries: The minimal-work function is \(m(p,\bar u)=\inf\{h:u(x,h)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h,\ x\ge 0,h\ge 0\}\). Utility is weakly decreasing in \(h\) and strictly increasing in goods.
question: Prove that \(m(p,\bar u)\) is nondecreasing in each component of \(p\).
solution: Take price vectors \(p'\ge p\) componentwise. If \((x,h)\) is feasible at prices \(p'\), then \(u(x,h)\ge \bar u\) and \(p'\cdot x\le h\). Since \(p\le p'\), we also have \(p\cdot x\le p'\cdot x\le h\). Thus every pair feasible at \(p'\) is feasible at \(p\). The feasible set at lower prices contains the feasible set at higher prices. Minimizing the same objective \(h\) over the larger feasible set cannot yield a larger value, so \(m(p,\bar u)\le m(p',\bar u)\). Therefore raising any goods price weakly increases minimal work.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the monotonicity proof.

title: A Counterexample to Homogeneity of Minimal Work
learning_objective: Show that minimal work need not be homogeneous of degree one when work enters utility.
preliminaries: Consider one consumption good with price \(p>0\). Work hours are \(h\ge 0\), the budget constraint is \(px\le h\), and the target utility is \(\bar u=1\). Utility is \(u(x,h)=x^2/h\) for \(h>0\).
question: Use this example to show that the minimal-work function need not be homogeneous of degree one in \(p\).
solution: At an optimum, \(h=px\); otherwise the consumer could reduce \(h\), which raises \(x^2/h\) and lowers the objective. The utility constraint becomes \[\frac{x^2}{px}=\frac{x}{p}\ge 1,\] so \(x\ge p\). Minimal work is therefore \[m(p,1)=\min_{x\ge p}px=p^2.\] If prices double, \(m(2p,1)=4p^2\), while degree-one homogeneity would require \(m(2p,1)=2m(p,1)=2p^2\). Thus the minimal-work function is not generally homogeneous of degree one in prices.

source.notes: Converted from the exam hint and source counterexample.

title: Homogeneity When Utility Is Independent of Work
learning_objective: Recover expenditure-function homogeneity when work hours do not affect utility.
preliminaries: Suppose utility is independent of work hours, so \(u(x,h)=v(x)\). The minimal-work function is \(m(p,\bar u)=\inf\{h:v(x)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h,\ x\ge 0,h\ge 0\}\).
question: Show that \(m(p,\bar u)\) is homogeneous of degree one in \(p\) when utility is constant in \(h\).
solution: If \(u(x,h)=v(x)\), the minimal-work problem reduces to \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0}\{p\cdot x:v(x)\ge \bar u\}.\] This is the standard expenditure problem, expressed in work-hours units because the wage is normalized to one. For any \(\lambda>0\), \[m(\lambda p,\bar u)=\inf_{x:v(x)\ge \bar u}\lambda p\cdot x=\lambda\inf_{x:v(x)\ge \bar u}p\cdot x=\lambda m(p,\bar u).\] Therefore the minimal-work function is homogeneous of degree one in prices under this additional assumption.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the expenditure-function homogeneity case.

title: Monopoly Pricing with Shutdown Demand
learning_objective: Solve monopoly pricing with a boundary case where marginal cost exceeds choke price.
preliminaries: A monopolist has constant marginal cost \(c_1>0\) and faces demand \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). Profit is \(\pi(p)=(p-c_1)D(p)\).
question: Find the monopoly price as \(c_1\) varies.
solution: If \(c_1\ge 1\), any price below one has negative margin and any price at least one yields zero demand and zero profit. Thus setting \(p=1\) is optimal. If \(0<c_1<1\), the firm maximizes \((p-c_1)(1-p)\) on \([c_1,1]\). The objective is strictly concave, and the FOC is \(1+c_1-2p=0\). Hence \(p^M=(1+c_1)/2\). Combining cases, \[p^M(c_1)=\begin{cases}(1+c_1)/2,&0<c_1\le 1,\\1,&c_1>1.\end{cases}\]

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the monopoly-pricing calculation.

title: Equal Prices in Pure Bertrand Equilibrium
learning_objective: Prove that pure equilibria in this asymmetric-cost Bertrand game must have equal prices.
preliminaries: Two firms sell a homogeneous good. Costs satisfy \(0<c_1<c_2\). Demand is \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). Consumers buy from the lower-price firm; if prices tie, firm 1 receives share \(\alpha_1\in[0,1]\). Firm 1's monopoly price is \(p_1^M\), and \(c_2<p_1^M\).
question: Show that any pure-strategy Nash equilibrium must have \(p_1=p_2\).
solution: Suppose first that \(p_1>p_2\). If \(p_2\ge c_2\), firm 1 gets no demand and can undercut to a price in \((c_1,p_2)\), earning positive profit. If \(p_2<c_2\), firm 2 earns negative profit when it sells and can raise to \(c_2\) to get nonnegative profit. Thus \(p_1>p_2\) is impossible. Suppose instead that \(p_1<p_2\). If \(p_1\ge c_2\), firm 2 can undercut firm 1 slightly and earn positive profit. If \(p_1<c_2\), firm 1 can raise price slightly while staying below \(\min\{p_2,c_2\}\), keeping all demand and increasing its margin. Thus \(p_1<p_2\) is impossible. Therefore any pure equilibrium must have equal prices.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the equal-prices argument.

title: Bertrand Equilibrium with Favorable Tie-Breaking
learning_objective: Verify the undominated pure equilibrium when ties favor the low-cost firm.
preliminaries: In the asymmetric-cost Bertrand game, \(0<c_1<c_2<p_1^M\), where \(p_1^M\) is firm 1's monopoly price. Demand is \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). If prices tie and \(\alpha_1=1\), firm 1 serves the whole market.
question: Find the unique pure-strategy Nash equilibrium in weakly undominated strategies when \(\alpha_1=1\).
solution: The equilibrium is \((p_1,p_2)=(c_2,c_2)\). Firm 1 serves the whole market and earns \((c_2-c_1)(1-c_2)>0\). Because \(c_2<p_1^M\), firm 1's monopoly profit is increasing at \(c_2\), so lowering price reduces profit, while raising price loses demand. Firm 2 earns zero. It cannot profitably lower price below \(c_2\), because that gives negative margin, and raising price gives zero demand and the same zero profit. Thus \((c_2,c_2)\) is a Nash equilibrium. In weakly undominated strategies it is unique: prices below \(c_2\) are weakly dominated for firm 2, and any common price above \(c_2\) can be profitably undercut by firm 2. Therefore \((c_2,c_2)\) is the unique pure equilibrium in weakly undominated strategies.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the alpha-one Bertrand equilibrium.

validation.notes: Deviation checks and undominated uniqueness verified.

title: No Pure Equilibrium with Unfavorable Tie-Breaking
learning_objective: Show why a pure equilibrium fails when the low-cost firm receives less than the full tie market.
preliminaries: Use the asymmetric-cost Bertrand model with \(0<c_1<c_2<p_1^M\). At equal prices, firm 1 receives market share \(\alpha_1\in[0,1)\). Any pure equilibrium must have equal prices.
question: Is there a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium when \(\alpha_1<1\)?
solution: No. Any pure equilibrium must have \(p_1=p_2=p\). If \(p<c_2\), firm 2 sells at negative margin or uses a weakly dominated below-cost price. If \(p>c_2\), firm 2 can slightly undercut and earn positive profit. If \(p=c_2\), firm 1 receives only share \(\alpha_1<1\) and earns \(\alpha_1(c_2-c_1)(1-c_2)\). By choosing \(c_2-\varepsilon\) for sufficiently small \(\varepsilon>0\), firm 1 captures the full market at almost the same margin and demand, earning strictly more because \(\alpha_1<1\). Thus no common price can be a Nash equilibrium, so no pure-strategy equilibrium exists.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the nonexistence proof.

title: Nonattainment in a Discontinuous Undercutting Problem
learning_objective: Explain why a best response may fail to exist because the profitable undercutting set is open.
preliminaries: Firm 1 has cost \(c_1\), firm 2 posts a finite price \(p_2>c_2>c_1\), and ties give firm 1 share \(\alpha_1<1\). Demand is \(D(p)=1-p\) for \(p\le 1\), and assume the relevant prices are below the choke price.
question: Does firm 1's profit maximization problem always have a solution against fixed \(p_2>c_2\) when \(\alpha_1<1\)?
solution: Not always. If firm 1 sets \(p_1<p_2\), it captures the market and earns \((p_1-c_1)(1-p_1)\). If it sets \(p_1=p_2\), it receives only share \(\alpha_1<1\). If the monopoly profit function is increasing up to \(p_2\), then firm 1 wants the highest price strictly below \(p_2\). The relevant problem is \[\max_{p_1\in[c_1,p_2)}(p_1-c_1)(1-p_1).\] This interval is open at \(p_2\). The supremum is approached as \(p_1\uparrow p_2\), but it is not attained. Pricing exactly \(p_2\) causes a discontinuous drop in demand share because \(\alpha_1<1\). Hence the best-response problem may have no solution.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the source nonattainment argument.

validation.notes: Open-set nonattainment logic checked under the stated increasing-profit condition.

title: Nash Equilibrium by Strict Dominance
learning_objective: Find the Nash equilibrium of a two-action simultaneous game.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses row \(S\) or \(C\); Player 2 chooses column \(S\) or \(C\). Payoffs are \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\), with Player 1's payoff first.
question: Find all Nash equilibria of the simultaneous-move game.
solution: For Player 1, \(C\) strictly dominates \(S\): against Player 2's \(S\), \(6>5\), and against Player 2's \(C\), \(4>3\). Thus Player 1 must choose \(C\) in any Nash equilibrium. Given \(C\), Player 2 compares \(S\), which gives \(3\), with \(C\), which gives \(4\). Player 2 chooses \(C\). Therefore the unique Nash equilibrium is \((C,C)\).

source.notes: Converted from the payoff matrix without relying on the source figure.

validation.notes: Strict dominance and best response check verified.

title: Stackelberg Equilibrium by Backward Induction
learning_objective: Solve the observed-action sequential version of a two-action game.
preliminaries: Use the payoff matrix \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\). Player 1 moves first; Player 2 observes Player 1's action and then chooses \(S\) or \(C\).
question: Find the subgame-perfect equilibrium of the sequential game.
solution: Use backward induction. If Player 1 chooses \(S\), Player 2 gets \(2\) from \(S\) and \(1\) from \(C\), so Player 2 chooses \(S\). If Player 1 chooses \(C\), Player 2 gets \(3\) from \(S\) and \(4\) from \(C\), so Player 2 chooses \(C\). Anticipating this, Player 1 compares \(5\) from choosing \(S\) with \(4\) from choosing \(C\). Player 1 chooses \(S\). The unique subgame-perfect equilibrium is: Player 1 chooses \(S\); Player 2 chooses \(S\) after \(S\) and \(C\) after \(C\).

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the Stackelberg solution.

title: Why Always Playing \(S\) Is Not a PBE
learning_objective: Check a proposed PBE by testing the leader's deviation incentive.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses \(S\) or \(C\). Player 2 observes a noisy signal and then chooses \(S\) or \(C\). Payoffs are \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\). The proposed strategy profile is Player 1 chooses \(S\), and Player 2 chooses \(S\) after every signal.
question: For \(0<\varepsilon<1\), is the proposed strategy profile a Perfect Bayesian equilibrium?
solution: No. Given beliefs that put probability one on Player 1 having chosen \(S\), Player 2 is willing to choose \(S\) after either signal, because against \(S\), Player 2 gets \(2\) from \(S\) and \(1\) from \(C\). But Player 1's strategy is not sequentially optimal given Player 2's strategy. If Player 1 chooses \(S\), Player 2 chooses \(S\), and Player 1 gets \(5\). If Player 1 deviates to \(C\), Player 2 still chooses \(S\) after every signal, and Player 1 gets \(6\). Since \(6>5\), Player 1 has a profitable deviation. Therefore the proposed profile is not a PBE for any \(0<\varepsilon<1\).

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the proposed-PBE rejection.

title: Bayesian Beliefs in a Noisy-Leader Game
learning_objective: Compute posterior beliefs after noisy signals and derive the follower's indifference condition.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses \(S\) with probability \(p\) and \(C\) with probability \(1-p\). The signal satisfies \(\Pr(s\mid S)=\Pr(c\mid C)=1-\varepsilon\). Player 2's payoffs are \(2\) from \((S,S)\), \(1\) from \((S,C)\), \(3\) from \((C,S)\), and \(4\) from \((C,C)\).
question: Compute \(\Pr(S\mid s)\) and \(\Pr(S\mid c)\). What value of \(p\) makes Player 2 indifferent after signal \(c\)?
solution: Bayes' rule gives \[\Pr(S\mid s)=\frac{(1-\varepsilon)p}{(1-\varepsilon)p+\varepsilon(1-p)}\] and \[\Pr(S\mid c)=\frac{\varepsilon p}{\varepsilon p+(1-\varepsilon)(1-p)}.\] After signal \(c\), Player 2's expected payoff from \(S\) is \(2\Pr(S\mid c)+3\Pr(C\mid c)\), and from \(C\) is \(1\Pr(S\mid c)+4\Pr(C\mid c)\). Indifference requires \(\Pr(S\mid c)=\Pr(C\mid c)\), which is equivalent to \[\varepsilon p=(1-\varepsilon)(1-p).\] Solving gives \(p=1-\varepsilon\).

source.locator: May 2025 Question 4(e) beliefs and follower indifference
source.notes: Subpart split from the mixed-PBE calculation.

validation.notes: Posterior formulas and follower indifference checked.

title: A Mixed PBE with Signal-Contingent Mixing
learning_objective: Construct a mixed PBE by combining follower and leader indifference conditions.
preliminaries: Use the noisy-leader game with payoffs \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), \((C,C)=(4,4)\), and signals satisfying \(\Pr(s\mid S)=\Pr(c\mid C)=1-\varepsilon\). Assume \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\).
question: Find a PBE where Player 2 plays \(S\) after signal \(s\) and mixes after signal \(c\).
solution: From Player 2's indifference after signal \(c\), Player 1 must choose \(S\) with probability \(p=1-\varepsilon\). This also makes Player 2 willing to play \(S\) after signal \(s\), since \(p\ge \varepsilon\). Let Player 2 choose \(S\) after signal \(c\) with probability \(q\). Player 1's expected payoff from \(S\) is \[(1-\varepsilon)5+\varepsilon(5q+3(1-q))=5-2\varepsilon(1-q).\] Player 1's expected payoff from \(C\) is \[\varepsilon 6+(1-\varepsilon)(6q+4(1-q))=4+2q+2\varepsilon(1-q).\] Indifference requires \[5-2\varepsilon(1-q)=4+2q+2\varepsilon(1-q),\] hence \[q=\frac{1-4\varepsilon}{2-4\varepsilon}.\] This lies in \((0,1)\) for \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\). The PBE is: Player 1 plays \(S\) with probability \(1-\varepsilon\); Player 2 plays \(S\) after \(s\); after \(c\), Player 2 plays \(S\) with probability \((1-4\varepsilon)/(2-4\varepsilon)\). Beliefs are determined by Bayes' rule on path.

source.notes: Subpart split focused on the final equilibrium construction.

validation.notes: Leader and follower mixing conditions checked for \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\).

title: No PBE with a Mixing Leader and Pure Follower
learning_objective: Rule out a class of PBEs by showing the leader cannot be indifferent.
preliminaries: In the noisy-leader game, Player 1's payoff is \(5\) at \((S,S)\), \(3\) at \((S,C)\), \(6\) at \((C,S)\), and \(4\) at \((C,C)\). A mixed strategy by Player 1 requires indifference between \(S\) and \(C\).
question: Can there be a PBE in which Player 1 mixes and Player 2 uses a pure action after every signal?
solution: No. If Player 2's pure strategy prescribes \(S\) after both signals, then Player 1 gets \(5\) from \(S\) and \(6\) from \(C\), so Player 1 strictly prefers \(C\). If Player 2's pure strategy prescribes \(C\) after both signals, then Player 1 gets \(3\) from \(S\) and \(4\) from \(C\), so Player 1 strictly prefers \(C\). If Player 2's pure strategy depends on the signal, Player 1's expected payoff from \(C\) exceeds that from \(S\) state by state by one whenever the same follower action is induced. In every pure follower strategy, Player 1 cannot be indifferent between \(S\) and \(C\). Therefore Player 1 cannot mix in a PBE with pure follower behavior.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the no-mixing-with-pure-follower result.

validation.notes: Leader payoff comparisons rule out indifference.

title: Positive Expected Return Need Not Attract a Log-Utility Investor
learning_objective: Show that positive expected monetary return is not sufficient for project acceptance under log utility.
preliminaries: An expected-utility maximizer has initial wealth \(1\) and Bernoulli utility \(u(w)=\log w\). A project pays \(\alpha>0\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(\beta>0\) with probability \(1/2\). Not investing leaves wealth \(1\).
question: Is \((\alpha+\beta)/2>1\) sufficient to guarantee that the individual accepts the all-or-nothing project?
solution: No. Expected utility from not investing is \(\log 1=0\). Expected utility from investing is \[\frac{1}{2}\log\alpha+\frac{1}{2}\log\beta=\log\sqrt{\alpha\beta}.\] The investor accepts only if \(\alpha\beta>1\). Positive expected return requires \(\alpha+\beta>2\), which does not imply \(\alpha\beta>1\). For example, \(\alpha=4\) and \(\beta=1/8\) give \((\alpha+\beta)/2=17/8>1\), but \(\alpha\beta=1/2<1\), so expected utility from investing is negative. Thus positive expected return is not sufficient.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion with explicit counterexample.

title: Necessary and Sufficient Acceptance Condition under Log Utility
learning_objective: Derive the exact acceptance condition for a binary risky project under log utility.
preliminaries: An investor has utility \(\log w\), initial wealth \(1\), and can either keep wealth \(1\) or invest in a project paying \(\alpha>0\) or \(\beta>0\), each with probability \(1/2\).
question: Give a necessary and sufficient condition on \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) for accepting the project strictly.
solution: The investor is indifferent to not investing at utility \(0\). Investing gives expected utility \[EU=\frac{1}{2}\log\alpha+\frac{1}{2}\log\beta=\log\sqrt{\alpha\beta}.\] Because \(\log\) is strictly increasing, \(EU>0\) if and only if \(\sqrt{\alpha\beta}>1\), equivalently \(\alpha\beta>1\). If \(\alpha\beta<1\), the project is rejected; if \(\alpha\beta=1\), the investor is indifferent. Thus strict acceptance is equivalent to \(\alpha\beta>1\).

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the exact expected-utility condition.

title: Positive Fractional Investment under Log Utility
learning_objective: Show that positive expected return implies a positive optimal investment share from zero.
preliminaries: The investor has initial wealth \(1\), utility \(\log w\), and may invest fraction \(\delta\in[0,1]\). Wealth is \(1-\delta+\delta\alpha\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(1-\delta+\delta\beta\) with probability \(1/2\). Assume \((\alpha+\beta)/2>1\) and wealth remains positive for small \(\delta\).
question: Will the investor choose a positive fraction of wealth to invest?
solution: Expected utility is \[V(\delta)=\frac{1}{2}\log(1+\delta(\alpha-1))+\frac{1}{2}\log(1+\delta(\beta-1)).\] At \(\delta=0\), \(V(0)=0\). The right derivative at zero is \[V'(0)=\frac{1}{2}(\alpha-1)+\frac{1}{2}(\beta-1)=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-1>0.\] Therefore there is some sufficiently small \(\delta>0\) such that \(V(\delta)>V(0)\). Hence \(\delta=0\) is not optimal, and any optimum invests a strictly positive fraction.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion; uses derivative-at-zero proof rather than the source's incomplete formula.

title: Counterexample to Truthfulness in a Two-Unit Second-Highest-Bid Auction
learning_objective: Disprove dominant-strategy truthfulness in a two-unit auction with second-highest-bid pricing.
preliminaries: There are two identical units and at least three unit-demand bidders. The two highest bidders each win one unit and each winner pays the second-highest bid. A bidder with value \(v_i\) who pays \(p\) receives payoff \(v_i-p\); a loser receives zero.
question: Is truthful bidding weakly dominant? If not, provide a counterexample.
solution: Truthful bidding is not weakly dominant. Take three bidders with values \(v_1=1/3\), \(v_2=1/2\), and \(v_3=1\). Suppose bidders 1 and 3 bid truthfully. If bidder 2 bids truthfully, the winning bids are \(1\) and \(1/2\), and the second-highest bid is \(1/2\). Bidder 2 wins and pays \(1/2\), giving payoff zero. If bidder 2 instead bids \(2/5\), the winning bids are \(1\) and \(2/5\), so bidder 2 still wins but pays \(2/5\). Her payoff is \(1/2-2/5=1/10>0\). Thus truthful bidding is not weakly dominant.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the source counterexample.

validation.notes: Counterexample checked under the stated payment rule.

title: Truthfulness in a Two-Unit Third-Price Auction
learning_objective: Prove weak dominance of truthful bidding in a two-unit third-price auction.
preliminaries: There are two identical units and at least three unit-demand bidders. The two highest bidders win and each pays the third-highest bid. Fix bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) and all opponents' bids. Let \(r\) be the second-highest bid among the opponents.
question: Is bidding \(b_i=v_i\) weakly dominant? Prove your answer.
solution: Yes. Bidder \(i\) wins exactly when her bid is high enough to enter the top two bids. The relevant threshold is \(r\), the second-highest opponent bid. If she wins, the third-highest overall bid is \(r\), so her payoff is \(v_i-r\). If she loses, her payoff is zero. If \(v_i>r\), truthful bidding wins and gives positive payoff, while losing would give zero and any other winning bid gives the same payoff. If \(v_i<r\), truthful bidding loses and gives zero, while any winning bid would require paying \(r>v_i\), giving negative payoff. If \(v_i=r\), winning and losing both give zero. Therefore truthful bidding is weakly optimal against every opponents' bid profile and is weakly dominant.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the third-price auction proof.

title: Writing the Consumer Problem with Possible Nonattainment
learning_objective: State the utility maximization problem carefully when an optimizer may not exist.
preliminaries: A consumer has wealth \(w>0\), price vector \(p\in\mathbb{R}_+^n\), and utility \(u:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}\). The budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Write the consumer's utility-maximization problem while accounting for the possibility that a maximum might not exist.
solution: The robust formulation uses a supremum: \[V(p,w)=\sup\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n,\ p\cdot x\le w\}.\] The demand correspondence is then \[X(p,w)=\arg\max\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n,\ p\cdot x\le w\}\] when the supremum is attained. If the supremum is not attained, \(X(p,w)\) is empty even though \(V(p,w)\) may still be well-defined as an extended real number.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the problem statement requirement.

title: Existence of Demand under Positive Prices
learning_objective: Prove existence of a utility maximizer when prices are strictly positive.
preliminaries: A consumer has continuous utility \(u:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}\), wealth \(w>0\), and price vector \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\). The budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\ge 0:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Prove that the consumer's problem has a solution. What can fail if prices are only nonnegative?
solution: If \(p_i>0\) for every \(i\), feasibility implies \(0\le x_i\le w/p_i\). Hence \(B(p,w)\) is bounded. It is closed as the intersection of \(\mathbb{R}_+^n\) and the closed half-space \(\{x:p\cdot x\le w\}\). Therefore \(B(p,w)\) is compact. By the Weierstrass extreme value theorem, continuous \(u\) attains a maximum on \(B(p,w)\). If some price is zero, the budget set can be unbounded. For example, if \(p_1=0\) and \(u(x)=x_1\), utility is unbounded above on the budget set, so no solution exists.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the existence proof.

title: Budget Exhaustion under Monotonicity
learning_objective: Prove that a monotone consumer spends all wealth at strictly positive prices.
preliminaries: Prices satisfy \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\), wealth is \(w>0\), and \(x^*\) is an optimal bundle. Strong monotonicity means \(x\ge y\) and \(x\ne y\) imply \(u(x)>u(y)\). Strict monotonicity means \(x\gg y\) implies \(u(x)>u(y)\).
question: Show that the budget constraint binds under strong monotonicity. Does the proof change if utility is only strictly increasing?
solution: Suppose \(p\cdot x^*<w\). Let \(\varepsilon=w-p\cdot x^*>0\), and define \[x'=x^*+\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sum_i p_i}(1,\ldots,1).\] Then \(x'\gg x^*\) and \[p\cdot x'=p\cdot x^*+\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sum_i p_i}\sum_i p_i=p\cdot x^*+\varepsilon/2<w.\] Thus \(x'\) is feasible and strictly preferred under strong monotonicity, contradicting optimality. The same construction also works under strict monotonicity, because \(x'\gg x^*\). Hence the budget binds under either condition.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the budget-binding proof.

title: Strict Quasiconcavity Implies Unique Demand
learning_objective: Prove single-valued demand from strict quasiconcavity on a convex budget set.
preliminaries: Let \(B(p,w)=\{x\ge 0:p\cdot x\le w\}\), with \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\). Utility is continuous and strictly quasiconcave: for distinct \(x,y\) and \(\lambda\in(0,1)\), \(u(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y)>\min\{u(x),u(y)\}\).
question: Assuming a maximizer exists, prove that the demand correspondence is single-valued. What changes if utility is not strongly increasing?
solution: Suppose two distinct bundles \(x\) and \(y\) both maximize utility on \(B(p,w)\), and let the maximum value be \(V\). Since the budget set is convex, \(z=\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y\) is feasible for \(\lambda\in(0,1)\). Strict quasiconcavity gives \(u(z)>\min\{u(x),u(y)\}=V\), a contradiction. Thus the maximizer is unique. Strong monotonicity is not needed for this uniqueness result. Without strong monotonicity, the unique bundle may fail to exhaust the budget, but strict quasiconcavity still rules out two distinct maximizers.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the uniqueness proof.

validation.notes: Convexity and strict quasiconcavity proof checked.

title: Why Demand Has No General Monotone Price Response
learning_objective: Explain why the standard consumer problem does not directly satisfy Milgrom-Shannon hypotheses.
preliminaries: Milgrom-Shannon monotone comparative statics requires ordered feasible sets with lattice structure and suitable single-crossing or increasing-differences conditions. A Walrasian budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Can we generally say how demand changes with prices under continuous, strongly increasing, strictly quasiconcave utility? Can Milgrom-Shannon's theorem be directly applied?
solution: There is no general coordinatewise monotonicity conclusion for Marshallian demand. A price increase combines substitution and wealth effects; own demand can even rise for a Giffen good, and cross-price effects can have either sign. The listed assumptions give existence, budget exhaustion, and uniqueness, but not monotone demand in prices. Milgrom-Shannon does not directly apply because the budget set is generally not a lattice. In two goods, two feasible bundles can have a componentwise join that violates the budget constraint. Therefore \(B(p,w)\) is not closed under joins and does not satisfy the required feasible-set structure. Demand comparative statics require other tools or stronger assumptions.

source.notes: Subpart-level conversion of the Milgrom-Shannon discussion.
